name: MoarVM MAST and Bytecode
summary: Raku's MoarVM has two bytecode levels — MAST (the high-level abstract bytecode that NQP and Raku compile to) and MoarVM bytecode (MBC, the lower-level executable form, register-based, with operations modeling Raku's rich semantics).

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
MoarVM (Raku's VM) has two bytecode levels. **MAST** (MoarVM Abstract Syntax Tree, despite the name a bytecode-shaped IR) is the high-level abstract bytecode that NQP and Raku compile to. **MoarVM bytecode** (often called MBC) is the lower-level executable form — register-based, with operations modeling Raku's rich semantics (multi-dispatch, multiple integer kinds, parameter handling, container types).

The two-level design lets MAST be the stable compile target while MBC evolves.

sections.title: Role in the Raku compile chain
sections.content:
The Raku source pipeline is RakuAST → QAST → MAST → MBC. Each step lowers to a more execution-shaped form. The architectural value of separating MAST from MBC is that MAST acts as a stable contract between the high-level Rakudo frontend and the lower-level VM-evolution surface — bytecode-format changes can happen at the MBC layer without forcing every consumer of MAST to update.

spesh and new-disp specialisation operate at the MBC level after first execution, producing tier-up to optimised form. See `data/moarvm-spesh` and `data/moarvm-new-disp` for the optimisation-pipeline angles.
